q: Modern vacuum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of <math> <mrow> <mtext>4.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> atm. at room temperature (300 K). Taking R = 8.3 JK<sup>-1</sup>  mole<sup>-1</sup>, 1 atm = 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>N</mtext> <mtext>Avogadro</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mo>mole</mo> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of :
question: Modern vacuum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of <math> <mrow> <mtext>4.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> atm. at room temperature (300 K). Taking R = 8.3 JK<sup>-1</sup>  mole<sup>-1</sup>, 1 atm = 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>N</mtext> <mtext>Avogadro</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mo>mole</mo> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of :
solution:
Let the inter molecular distance be D
Then in a volume of 4 3 π D 3 there is only one molecule.

4 3 π D 3 P=( 1 N A )RT

D= ( 3RT 4π N A P ) 1 3

Substituting P=4× 10 −10 Pa,  R=8.3,   N A =6× 10 23 ,  T=300K

D=0.2 mm

If during this process the relation of pressure P and volume V is given by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant, then n is given by (Here <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow></msub></math> are molar specific heat at constant pressure and constant volume, respectively) :
question: An ideal gas undergoes a quasi-static, reversible process in which its molar heat capacity C remains constant. If during this process the relation of pressure P and volume V is given by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant, then n is given by (Here <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow></msub></math> are molar specific heat at constant pressure and constant volume, respectively) :
solution:
PVn=k

C=Cv+R1-n ; C-Cv=R1-n

1-n=RC-Cv ; n=1-RC-Cv

n=C-Cv-RC-Cv ; n=C-Cv-Cp-CvC-Cv

n=C-Cv-Cp+CvC-Cv ; n=C-CpC-Cv

q: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> moles of argon and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> moles of oxygen at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
question: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> moles of argon and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> moles of oxygen at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
solution:
As we know, internal energy is given by, U=nCvT. Also argon gas is monoatomic and oxygen gas is diatomic.

Therefore, total internal energy will beUnet=n1CV1T+n2CV2T

=8×3R2×T+6×5R2×T

=27RT

solution:
V=2 m3

P=3×106 Pa

PV=nRT

E=f2nRT=f2PV=32×3×106×2

=9×106J

q:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
question:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
solution:
The RMS speed is given by 

vrms=3RTM

The average speed is given by 

vavg=8RTπM

The ratio is 

⇒vrmsvavg =3RTM8RTπM=3π8=38×227⇒vrmsvavg =3328

Comparing it with the given value 1+5x12,

vrmsvavg =1+528⇒x=28

subject: Physics
q: One kg of a diatomic gas is at a pressure of $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The density of the gas is $4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{-3}$. What is the energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
question: One kg of a diatomic gas is at a pressure of $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The density of the gas is $4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{-3}$. What is the energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
solution:
Thermal energy corresponds to internal energy
Mass $=1 \mathrm{~kg}$
density $=8 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$
$\Rightarrow$ Volume $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1}{8} \mathrm{~m}^3$
Pressure $=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$
$\therefore$ Internal Energy $=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{V}=5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q: A metal ball of mass $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is heated upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and dropped into a vessel of heat capacity $800 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ and containing $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$ water. The initial temperature of water and vessel is $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the approximate percentage increment in the temperature of the water? [Specific Heat Capacities of water and metal are, respectively, $4200 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-}$ ${ }^{1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ and $400 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]
question: A metal ball of mass $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is heated upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and dropped into a vessel of heat capacity $800 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ and containing $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$ water. The initial temperature of water and vessel is $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the approximate percentage increment in the temperature of the water? [Specific Heat Capacities of water and metal are, respectively, $4200 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-}$ ${ }^{1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ and $400 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]
solution:
Assume final temperature $=\mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Heat lass $=$ Heat gain $=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{B}} \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{w}} \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{w}}$

$0.1 \times 400 \times(500-\mathrm{T})$

$\quad=0.5 \times 4200 \times(\mathrm{T}-30)+800(\mathrm{~T}-30)$

$\Rightarrow 40(500-\mathrm{T})=(\mathrm{T}-30)(2100+800)$

$\Rightarrow 20000-40 \mathrm{~T}=2900 \mathrm{~T}-30 \times 2900$

$\Rightarrow 20000+30 \times 2900=\mathrm{T}(2940)$

$\mathrm{T}=30.4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}} \times 100=\frac{6.4}{30} \times 100=21 \%$

so the closest answer is $20 \%$.

f=4+3+3=10, assuming non-linear, 

β=CpCv=1+2f=1210=1.2

question: The root-mean-square speed of molecules of a given mass of a gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> atmosphere pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The root-mean-square speed of molecules of the gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>127</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> atmosphere pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be __________.
solution:
vrms=3RTM⇒vrms∝T⇒vrms2vrms1=T2 T1=400300=23

vrms2=23vrms1=23×200⇒vrms2=4003 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Statement I: Change in internal energy of a system containing $n$ mole of ideal gas can be written as $\Delta U = n C_v (T_f - T_i) = \dfrac{nR}{\gamma - 1}(T_f - T_i)$, where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, $T_i =$ initial temperature, $T_f =$ final temperature.
Statement II: Relation between degree of freedom $f$ and $\gamma (= C_p/C_v)$ is $\left(\gamma = 1 + \dfrac{2}{f}\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Statement I: Change in internal energy of a system containing $n$ mole of ideal gas can be written as $\Delta U = n C_v (T_f - T_i) = \dfrac{nR}{\gamma - 1}(T_f - T_i)$, where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, $T_i =$ initial temperature, $T_f =$ final temperature.
Statement II: Relation between degree of freedom $f$ and $\gamma (= C_p/C_v)$ is $\left(\gamma = 1 + \dfrac{2}{f}\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
Statement I is true because for an ideal gas, the change in internal energy is given by $\Delta U = n C_v \Delta T$. Using Mayer's relation $C_p - C_v = R$ and the ratio of specific heats $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, we can write $C_v = \dfrac{R}{\gamma - 1}$. Substituting this, we get $\Delta U = \dfrac{nR}{\gamma - 1}(T_f - T_i)$.

Statement II is true because according to the law of equipartition of energy, the molar heat capacity at constant volume is $C_v = \dfrac{f}{2}R$ and at constant pressure is $C_p = \left(\dfrac{f}{2} + 1\right)R$. Therefore, $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = 1 + \dfrac{2}{f}$.

Statement II does not explain Statement I, as Statement I is derived purely from macroscopic thermodynamic relations (Mayer's relation) and is independent of the microscopic concept of degrees of freedom.

Hence, both statements are true but Statement II is NOT the correct explanation of Statement I.

Answer: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The temperature of a gas is -73 °C. When the gas is heated to 527 °C, the root mean square speed of the molecules is doubled.
Statement II : The product of pressure and volume of an ideal gas will be equal to translational kinetic energy of the molecules.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The temperature of a gas is -73 °C. When the gas is heated to 527 °C, the root mean square speed of the molecules is doubled.
Statement II : The product of pressure and volume of an ideal gas will be equal to translational kinetic energy of the molecules.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Given:

T1=-73 °C=-73+273 K=200 K

and T2=527°C=527+273 K=800 K

Therefore,

Vrms, 1Vrms, 2=3RT1M3RT2M=T1T2

=200800=12

Vrms, 2=2 Vrms, 1

Hence,

Statement-I is true.

Now,

PV=nRT

But translational KE=32nRT

Hence, Statement-II is false.

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of a monoatomic gas is at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The density of the gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the order of energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of a monoatomic gas is at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The density of the gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the order of energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
solution:
Internal energy is the energy due to thermal motion.

The internal energy of an ideal gas given by

U=f2nRT (for n moles)

U=f2PV=f2×P×Mρ (Since, f=3  for a monoatomic gas.)

U=32×4×104×28

=1.5×104J
So, the order of energy due to thermal motion is 104

q: Using equipartition of energy, the specific heat (in J kg-1 K-1 ) of Aluminium at high temperature can be estimated to be (atomic weight of Aluminium =27)
question: Using equipartition of energy, the specific heat (in J kg-1 K-1 ) of Aluminium at high temperature can be estimated to be (atomic weight of Aluminium =27)
solution:
Solid atom possesses 6 degrees of freedom due to vibrations at high temperature.

Cv=12fR=6R2=3R for 1 mole
∴ Specific heat capacity in J kg-1 K-1=CvM

=3RM=3×8.31427×1000

≈925

q: What will be the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)
question: What will be the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)
solution:
Energy associated with each degree of freedom per molecule =12kBT.

Hence, the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature T =12kBT.

subject: Physics
q: If $2$ mole of an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$, is mixed with $6$ mole of another ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $2T$ then the temperature of mixture is :
question: If $2$ mole of an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$, is mixed with $6$ mole of another ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $2T$ then the temperature of mixture is :
solution:
Using the principle of conservation of internal energy for the mixture of ideal gases:

$U_{mix} = U_1 + U_2$

$(n_1 + n_2) C_{V} T_{mix} = n_1 C_{V} T_1 + n_2 C_{V} T_2$

Since both gases are monoatomic, their molar heat capacities at constant volume are equal ($C_{V} = \dfrac{3}{2}R$).

$(n_1 + n_2) T_{mix} = n_1 T_1 + n_2 T_2$

Substituting the given values $n_1 = 2$, $T_1 = T$, $n_2 = 6$, and $T_2 = 2T$:

$(2 + 6) T_{mix} = 2(T) + 6(2T)$

$8 T_{mix} = 2T + 12T$

$8 T_{mix} = 14T$

$T_{mix} = \dfrac{14}{8}T = \dfrac{7}{4}T$

Answer: $\dfrac{7}{4}T$

subject: Physics
q:
Match the $\mathrm {List-I}$ with $\mathrm {List-II}$
$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}\hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Triatomic rigid gas} & \text{I.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{5}{3} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \begin{array}{l} \text{Diatomic non-rigid} \\ \text{gas} \end{array} & \text{II.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{7}{5} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Monoatomic gas} & \text{III.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{4}{3} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{Diatomic rigid gas} & \text{IV }& \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{9}{7} \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the $\mathrm {correct}$ answer from the options given below :
question:
Match the $\mathrm {List-I}$ with $\mathrm {List-II}$
$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}\hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Triatomic rigid gas} & \text{I.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{5}{3} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \begin{array}{l} \text{Diatomic non-rigid} \\ \text{gas} \end{array} & \text{II.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{7}{5} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Monoatomic gas} & \text{III.} & \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{4}{3} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{Diatomic rigid gas} & \text{IV }& \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{9}{7} \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the $\mathrm {correct}$ answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{f}}$
$f=6$, Triatomic rigid gas
$\mathrm{f}=7$, Diatomic non-rigid gas
$f=5$, Diatomic rigid gas
$\mathrm{f}=3$, monoatomic rigid gas
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{6}=\frac{4}{3}$ (Triatomic)
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{7}=\frac{9}{7}($ Diatomic, non-rigid $)$
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{5}=\frac{7}{5}($ Diatomic, rigid $)$
$\gamma=1+\frac{2}{3}=\frac{5}{3}($ Monoatomic, rigid $)$
A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

subject: Physics
q: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
question: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
solution:
Given,

Temperature of Nitrogen gas is 

T1=300 °C=300+273=573 K

Molar mass of H2 gas is MH2=2 g

Molar mass of N2 gas is MN2=28 g

The rms speed of gas is given by

 Vrms=3RTM

Now rms speed of hydrogen gas is equal to rms speed of nitrogen gas, i.e.; VrmsH2=VrmsN2
⇒TH2TN2=MH2MN2

Substitute the given values i above equation, we get,

TH2=228×573

TH2=41 K

subject: Physics
q: At which temperature the r.m.s. velocity of a hydrogen molecule equal to that of an oxygen molecule at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> ?
question: At which temperature the r.m.s. velocity of a hydrogen molecule equal to that of an oxygen molecule at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>47</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> ?
solution:
The r.m.s velocity of the molecule of a gas of molecular weight M at Kelvin temperature T is given by, Vrms=3RTM.

Let MO and MH are molecular weight of oxygen and hydrogen and TO and TH the corresponding Kelvin temperature at which Vr.m.s is same for both,

Vr.m.s=3RTOMO=3RTHMH. Hence TOMO=THMH

TO=273°C+47°C=320 K, 

MO=32, 

MH=2⇒TH=232×320=20 K

subject: Physics
q: If the R.M.S. speed of oxygen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the R.M.S. speed of hydrogen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
question: If the R.M.S. speed of oxygen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the R.M.S. speed of hydrogen molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
solution:
Recall the formula of root mean square speed, vRMS=3RTM, 

⇒vRMSO2 vRMSH2=MH2 MO2=232=14

⇒vRMSH2=4 vRMSO2=4×160=640 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A polyatomic ideal gas has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> vibrational modes. What is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: A polyatomic ideal gas has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> vibrational modes. What is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
Since each vibrational mode has 2 degrees of freedom hence total vibrational degrees of freedom =48

f=3+3+48=54

γ=1+2f=2827=1.03

q: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math>, travelling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> strikes a fixed wooden target. One half of its kinetic energy is converted into heat in the wood. The rise of temperature of the bullet if the specific heat of its material is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>030</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>gram</mi><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>ergs</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> close to :
question: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math>, travelling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> strikes a fixed wooden target. One half of its kinetic energy is converted into heat in the wood. The rise of temperature of the bullet if the specific heat of its material is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>030</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>gram</mi><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>ergs</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> close to :
solution:
As per given condition Half of kinetic energy is transferred into heat energy and raises the temperature of bullet

12×12mv2=msΔT

Δt=V24s

=210×2104×4.2×0.3×1000=87.5°C

subject: Physics
q: For a particular ideal gas which of the following graphs represents the variation of mean squared velocity of the gas molecules with temperature?
question: For a particular ideal gas which of the following graphs represents the variation of mean squared velocity of the gas molecules with temperature?
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{ms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}} \\
& \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2=3 \mathrm{RT} / \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$

Hence we can conclude that $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2$ is directly proportional to temperature
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}$
$\Rightarrow$ Graph will be straight line

q: What will be the effect on the root mean square velocity of oxygen molecules if the temperature is doubled and oxygen molecule dissociates into atomic oxygen?
question: What will be the effect on the root mean square velocity of oxygen molecules if the temperature is doubled and oxygen molecule dissociates into atomic oxygen?
options: The velocity of atomic oxygen remains same
The velocity of atomic oxygen doubles
The velocity of atomic oxygen becomes half
The velocity of atomic oxygen becomes four times
solution:
The RMS velocity of a gas is given by, vRMS=3RTM

When O2 dissociates into O its molecular mass becomes M2. Therefore,

Ratio of RMS speed, v1v2=T1T2×M2M1⇒v1v2=T2T×M/2M⇒v2v1=2

Assume the molecular diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and the gas is ideal. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>

Assume the molecular diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and the gas is ideal. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
λ=RT2πd2 NAP

λ=8.314×3002×3.14×0.3×10-92×6.023×1023×1.01×105

λ=102 nm

q: Two vessels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are of the same size and are at same temperature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are the pressures of the gases in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
question: Two vessels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are of the same size and are at same temperature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are the pressures of the gases in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
For both the gases, it follows that

PAVAPBVB=nARTAnBRTB   ...1

Given VA=VB and TA=TB.

Thus, from equation (1), it follows that

PAPB=nAnB=12132=16

subject: Physics
q:
Following statements are given
(1) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases when the temperature is reduced.
(2) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule increases with increase in pressure at constant temperature.
(3) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases with increases in volume.
(4) Pressure of a gas increases with increase in temperature at constant volume.
(5) The volume of gas decreases with increase in temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Following statements are given
(1) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases when the temperature is reduced.
(2) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule increases with increase in pressure at constant temperature.
(3) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases with increases in volume.
(4) Pressure of a gas increases with increase in temperature at constant volume.
(5) The volume of gas decreases with increase in temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Average kinetic energy of a gas molecule is given by KEavg =32KT.

Pressure of a gas in terms of rms speed is given by P=13ρvrms2.

As KEavg∝T, so 1 is correct, 2 is incorrect, statement 3 can not be said.

Note : Statement (4) is correct only if we consider it at constant volume and not constant pressure. Ideally, this question must be bonus but most appropriate answer is option (A)
